coredata.dc:title: Optical fiber plasmonic sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of copper (II) ion based on trimetallic Au@AgPt core-shell nanospheres
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Qing
Zhu, Wenjie
Wang, Yong
Deng, Zhao
Li, Zhi
Peng, Jiankun
Lyu, Dajuan
Lewis, Elfed
Yang, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: This article presents for the first time an ultrasensitive localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) sensor system based on multimode-singlemode-multimode fiber (MMF-SMF-MMF) structure for the accurate measurement of copper (II) Ion (Cu2+). Citrate-stabilized Au@AgPt core-shell nanospheres (NSs) with a rough AgPt alloy outer shell were synthesized in house, and then adhered onto the sensing section of MMF-SMF-MMF structure through silane coupling agent. Finally, the Au@AgPt NSs-coated optical fiber probe surface was covered with a layer of PEI as the chelating agent for specific Cu2+ trapping. The detection process of Cu2+ is conducted by monitoring the resonance intensity variation of the transmission spectrum, which is due to the local refractive index changes result- ing from the specific interactions between Cu2+ in solution and PEI immobilized on the sensor surface. The proposed PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified fiber sensor achieved an ultralow Cu2+ detection level down to 10−16 mol/L. The facile fabrication, pollution-free, high sensitivity and cost-effectiveness of the optical fiber sensor system make it rather promising for the precise Cu2+ monitoring in a wide range of biological application scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR)
Au@AgPt nanospheres
Refractive index
Optical fiber sensor
Polyethylenimine (PEI)
Copper (II) ion (Cu2+)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the plasmonic sensors coated with nanospheres
Refractive index measurement and metal ion detection
RI sensitivity of Au@AgPt NSs modified multimode-singlemode-multimode fiber (MMF-SMF-MMF) structure
PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure for the quantitative and selective detection of Cu2+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of fabrication process of PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF probe for Cu2+ detection. (b) Experimental setup for LSPR refractive index sensing and Cu2+ detection.

(a) is the selected area HAADF-STEM images of Au@AgPt NSs. (b) is the selected area energy disperse spectroscopy mapping images of Au@AgPt NSs. (c) is the elements distribution images of the Au@AgPt NSs. The red, blue and green colors in (c) represent gold, silver, and platinum elements, respectively. All scale bars are 20 nm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(a) LSPR transmission spectra of Au@AgPt NSs modified fiber RI sensor. (b) The transmission spectrum resonance intensity value change as a function of RI.

(a) Low resolution SEM images of PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure. (b) High resolution SEM images of PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure. (c) The dip in transmittance of Cu2+ detection in the concentration range of 10−16 to 10-10 mol/L. (d) Calibration curve of transmission change at 564 nm versus Cu2+ concentration.

Selectivity of the PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure for Cu2+ detection. Mg2+, Mn2+, Sn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe3+ and Zn2+ were each tested at the concentrations of 10−13 mol/L in sodium citrate-citrate buffer solution at pH 4.0.
Comparison of different methods for the Cu2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Optical fiber plasmonic sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of copper (II) ion based on trimetallic Au@AgPt core-shell nanospheres

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Fiber Optical Sensing Technology, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Fiber Optical Sensing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Laboratory for Fiber Optical Sensing Technology, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Optical Fibre Sensors Research Centre, University of Limerick, Limerick, V94 T9PX, Ireland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Optical Fibre Sensors Research Centre, University of Limerick, Limerick, Ireland. V94 T9PX

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article presents for the first time an ultrasensitive localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) sensor system based on multimode-singlemode-multimode fiber (MMF-SMF-MMF) structure for the accurate measurement of copper (II) Ion (Cu2+). Citrate-stabilized Au@AgPt core-shell nanospheres (NSs) with a rough AgPt alloy outer shell were synthesized in house, and then adhered onto the sensing section of MMF-SMF-MMF structure through silane coupling agent. Finally, the Au@AgPt NSs-coated optical fiber probe surface was covered with a layer of PEI as the chelating agent for specific Cu2+ trapping. The detection process of Cu2+ is conducted by monitoring the resonance intensity variation of the transmission spectrum, which is due to the local refractive index changes result- ing from the specific interactions between Cu2+ in solution and PEI immobilized on the sensor surface. The proposed PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified fiber sensor achieved an ultralow Cu2+ detection level down to 10−16 mol/L. The facile fabrication, pollution-free, high sensitivity and cost-effectiveness of the optical fiber sensor system make it rather promising for the precise Cu2+ monitoring in a wide range of biological application scenarios.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is an essential trace element and a component of many important enzymes in living organisms, which can promote the absorption and utilization of iron and maintain the functions of the central nervous system. When the amount of copper ions (Cu2+) ingested in the human body is excessive, it is easy to over-burden the liver, gallbladder and other organs in the body, which makes the human body metabolism disordered, resulting in necrotic hepatitis and hemolytic anemia [1,2]. Therefore, the pollution-free, highly sensitivity and cost-effective designs for the recognition and trace analysis of Cu2+ are extremely important in environmental science and medical monitoring. Classic analytical methods for Cu2+ detection mainly include fluorescence spectrometry [3–5], colorimetry [6–8], electrochemistry [9,10], photothermal effect [11] and localized surface plasmon resonance [12,13]. Among all the above, localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) has been broadly widely recognized as one of the most cost-effective and convenient tools for rapid and ultra-sensitive Cu2+ detection.

LSPR is derived from the resonance of collective oscillation interaction between the incident electromagnetic wave and free electrons in the conduction band of metallic nanoparticles (NPs) at resonant frequency, which in turn leads to local electromagnetic (EM) fields amplification and an intense optical extinction band [14]. This enhanced electric field decays sharply with the increase of the distance away from the surface of the particle [15]. This phenomenon supplies an effective platform for measuring the changes of the refractive index (RI) near the surfaces of noble metal NPs. The RI change of the medium around the plasmonic NPs leads to a significant variation of the plasmon extinction band [16]. Generally, the wavelength change or the dip in transmission is approximately in proportional to the amount of analytes present in the surrounding environment [17].

LSPR sensor system can be designed for measuring a wide range of parameters including refractive index [18], hydrogen [19,20], DNA [21], protein [22] and heavy metal ions [23]. Liu et al. reported a LSPR sensor of Cu2+ detection based on the polyethylenimine (PEI)-capped gold nanorods, and the detection limit was determined as 2.4 × 10−7 mol/L [24]. Hu et al. developed a copper (II) ion sensor based on the amino-functionalized graphitic carbon encapsulated gold nanoparticles, achieving a detection limit of 10-8 mol/L [13]. However, the LSPR sensors described above are mostly conducted in aqueous solution, which are highly susceptible to the interference of the complex ion components from the surrounding environment, and lead to inaccurate analysis of experimental data and low detection sensitivity. In order to improve the specific sensitivity of the Cu2+, LSPR sensors mediated with novel plasmonic NPs based on optical fiber structure could be an ideal solution. By exploiting the enhanced light-matter interactions between plasmonic NPs and the evanescent field, LSPR optical fiber sensor is emerging as a vital tool for biochemical sensing [25–30]. To achieve and enhance the evanescent field for higher sensitivity, optical fiber structure, such as cladding-removed fiber [25], side-polished/D-shaped fiber [26,27], tapered optical fiber [28], U-shaped fiber [29], and fiber Bragg grating [30] are usually further fabricated. Since these optical fibers inevitably require the complete removal of the cladding region or at least making it thinner by mechanical polishing or chemical dissolution, it greatly gives rise to complicated fabrication processes and inferior mechanical robustness. To get rid of this, hetero-core spliced optical fibers have been developed with promising characteristics for SPR sensing, such as strong evanescent field, miniature design, excellent biocompatibility and high stability [31,32]. The multimode-singlemode-multimode fiber (MMF-SMF-MMF) structure was firstly proposed by Iga et al., where the cladding layer of singlemode fiber (SMF) worked as a sensing surface, and a layer of silver film was coated on surface of the SMF for refractive index detection [31]. Especially, MMF-SMF-MMF structure spliced with two types of fibers with different core diameter has been proposed to deliberately leak the transmitted light wave into the cladding layer at the interfaces of hetero-core splicing. The localized surface plasmon wave is thereby excited due to the existence of an evanescent wave which in turn interact with deposited gold/silver NPs on the cladding surface.

This article proposed for the first time that trimetallic nanoparticles modified multimode-singlemode-multimode fiber (MMF-SMF-MMF) structure for the accurate Cu2+ detection. The positively charged polyethylenimine (PEI) was successively adsorbed onto the surface of Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure through electrostatic interaction. The Cu2+ measurement was achieved by monitoring the transmission spectrum while satisfying the resonance condition. This method not only achieve specific and long-distance Cu2+ monitoring, but also greatly improves the lower detection limit of Cu2+ down to 10−16 mol/L, which demonstrates promising applications in environmental and biological fields.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O, 99.99 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8 %), hexachloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O, 99.8 %), trisodium citrate dehydrate, citric acid monohydrate, ethanol absolute, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) and glycerol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95 %–98 %) was bought from Xinyang Chemical Reagent Factory (Henan, China). 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTES, 99 %) was obtained from Shandong Usolf Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Polyethylenimine (PEI, 50 %, wv%, Mw = 70, 000) was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Metal salts of CuCl2·2H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, SnCl2·2H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CaCl2, BaCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, ZnCl2·2H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Metal ion solutions with different concentrations were prepared using a sodium citrate-citrate buffer solution (0.1 mol/l, pH = 4). All reagents were employed as received. In all experiments, deionized (DI) water (18 MΩ) was used. Single-mode Step Index (SI) (SMF) fibers and multi-mode Graded Index (GI) fibers (MMF) were supplied by YOFC Ltd (China). The core diameters of the SMF and MMF were 9 μm and 62.5 μm, respectively.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the nanospheres were recorded using a UV-2400 spectrophotometer in a range of 190−1100 nm. The topographic images and the energy disperse X-ray (EDX) mapping analysis of these nanospheres were obtained using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Talos F200S, Thermo Fisher, USA). The surface morphology of Au@AgPt NSs and PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure were checked using a Zeiss Ultra Plus Field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss, German). The Zeta potentials of Au@AgPt NSs solution were determined using a Malvern Zetasizer ZEN 3600 analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, U.K.).

Briefly, 50 mL of 2.94 × 10−4 M HAuCl4·3H2O solution was boiled at 100 °C under vigorous stirring, then 3 mL of 3.88 × 10-2 M sodium tricitrate solution was added instantaneously into the solution. After boiling for 25 min, 6 mL of 5.88 × 10-3 M silver nitrate solution was then added and following with 6.8 mL of 3.88 × 10-2 M sodium tricitrate solution was subsequently added. After boiling sequentially for 1 h, 30 mL of Au@Ag NSs were mixed with 210 μL of 3.86 × 10-2 M H2PtCl6·6H2O, and stirring continued for a further 30 min. The mixture solutions turned to fuchsia immediately after addition of H2PtCl6·6H2O indicating the formation of Au@AgPt NSs. Negatively charged citrate ions were wrapped on the surface of the Au@AgPt NSs, and the surface of Au@AgPt NSs were negatively charged, which was indicated by the zeta potential at around -60.7 mV and -51.4 mV (Table S2) before and after centrifugation of the Au@AgPt NSs solution, respectively. Therefore, the Au@AgPt NSs in aqueous solution remain dispersed because the negatively charged citrate ions are wrapped on the surface of the Au@AgPt NSs and trisodium citrate dehydrate acts as a stabilizer [33–35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the plasmonic sensors coated with nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 (a) illustrates the fabrication process of PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF probe for Cu2+ detection. The preparation of MMF-SMF-MMF structure was documented in our previous reports [36]. Briefly, the MMF-SMF-MMF structure in this work was fabricated with a 15 mm single-mode SI fiber (SMF) inserted between two multi-mode GI fibers (MMF), which were connected using standard thermal fusion splicing (FSM-60 s, Fujikura Ltd. Japan)after cleaving each end of fiber with a standard fiber cleaver (CT-30, Fujikura Ltd. Japan). The 15 mm-long section of SMF was selected for sensing. Prior to immobilization of the nanospheres onto the sensing interface, these nanospheres solution were centrifuged twice, then these nanospheres solid residues were dispersed in a suitable volume of distilled water for further use. The SMF sensing sections were firstly immersed in a piranha solution (H2SO4 and H2O2 in 3:7 vol ratio) for 30 min to be activated with reactive hydroxyl groups on the surface and then rinsed 3 times with DI water. Subsequently, the fibers were immersed in an APTMS-elthanol solution for 10 min to achieve amino-functionalization. Then the sensing sections were thoroughly rinsed 3 times using ethanol and placed in an oven at 120 °C for 2 h to activate the silanization process. After that, the APTES-modified fibers were immersed in the as-prepared Au@AgPt NSs solution for 23 s and then the fibers were removed from the solution and thoroughly rinsed with deionized (DI) water. Finally, the positively charged PEI was successively adsorbed on the negatively charged Au@AgPt NSs surface by electrostatic interactiuons. The PEI solution was diluted to a concentration of 10 mg/mL in water. After 60 min, PEI was electrostatically self-assembled onto the Au@AgPt NSs surface to form a membrane. Then, the sensing section was rinsed using sodium citrate-citrate buffer solution (0.1 mol/l, pH = 4) to remove any unreacted PEI residues. After pure nitrogen (N2) drying, the transmission spectra were recorded in air and used as the reference data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Refractive index measurement and metal ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental setup for the fiber-optic LSPR refractive index sensing and Cu2+ detection is illustrated in Fig. 1(b). The light source input light was provided using a tungsten-halogen lamp (broadband visible/ Near Infrared) light source (360−2,000 nm), and the transmission spectra were monitored and acquired using an optical fiber spectrometer (Flame-T-VIS-NIR, 350−1,010 nm, Ocean Optics Ltd.). The refractive index sensitivity of MMF-SMF-MMF structure coated with Au@AgPt NSs was determined by measuring the intensity variation of the transmission spectra. The sensing section was rinsed thoroughly with deionized water before each measurement. As for Cu2+ detection, the surface of a newly prepared PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure was immersed in 700 μL of different concentrations of Cu2+ for 10 min, and the unreacted Cu2+ solution was thoroughly washed off in citrate buffer sodium (0.1 mol/l, pH = 4) after each measurement. Finally, the transmission spectra was recorded in the citrate buffer sodium (0.1 mol/l, pH = 4) after the Cu2+ detection. Standard buffered Cu2+ solutions with different concentrations of 10−16, 10-15, 10-14, 10-13, 10-12, 10-11, 10-10 mol/L were prepared in a citrate buffer (sodium-citrate citrate, 0.1 mol/l, pH = 4) for the concentration-dependent measurements. All test experiments were performed at the temperature of 23 ± 0.1 °C.

The high angle annular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images of the selected area were performed to characterize the elemental composition of Au@AgPt NSs in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2(a), there is a significant difference in the brightness between the inner and outer regions of the Au@AgPt NSs, which confirms that the nanospheres with core-shell structure have been successfully obtained. The displacement reaction between Ag and H2PtCl6 at room temperature can be described as follows: 4 A g
+
P t C l
6
2 -
→ P t
+ 4 A g C l
+ 2
C l
-
. AgCl may adhere to the surface of the core particles and can roughen the surface of the particles [37]. However, the galvanic replacement at the temperature of 100 °C can prevent the formation of AgCl precipitates from interfering with the electrical replacement process, resulting in a smooth Pt shell [38,39]. The discontinuous platinum shell could be owing to the competition and the synergistic effect of galvanic replacement between Ag and H2PtCl6·6H2O and the chemical reduction reaction between Na3Ct and H2PtCl6·6H2O under 100 °C [40]. Fig. 2(b) is the selected area energy disperse X-ray (EDX) mapping image of Au@AgPt NSs. The red, blue and green colors represent gold, silver, and platinum elements, respectively. Au is mainly distributed in the center, whereas Ag and Pt are distributed throughout the nanospheres including the shell, which implies the formation of Au@AgPt core-shell nanospheres. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the line-scan elements distribution data of Au@AgPt NSs were obtained from single Au@AgPt NS in Fig. 2(a), respectively. The Au element signal in Au@AgPt NSs is limited to the central region with a scale range of about 15 nm, while the signals of Ag and Pt components are distributed across the entire nanoparticles, indicating the formation of the alloy structure AgPt shell, the mass fractions of Pt and Ag in the AgPt alloy shell are 56.55 % and 43.45 %, respectively. The alloying process is caused by the rapid diffusion of Ag and Pt atoms at high temperature [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: RI sensitivity of Au@AgPt NSs modified multimode-singlemode-multimode fiber (MMF-SMF-MMF) structure
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a) shows the sensitivity of Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure to the surrounding refractive index, the grow time of Au@AgPt NSs on the APTES-modified fiber was 23 s. The sensing surface was immersed into different glycerin concentrations. The depth of the transmission peak gradually becomes greater when the RI of the external solution increases. Since the RI sensitivity of Au@AgPt NSs modified fiber is affected not only by the optical parameters of the surrounding medium, but also by the interparticle gap distance of the nanoparticles deposited on the sensing surface. When the growth time of Au@AgPt NSs is too long, Au@AgPt NSs modified on the fiber will be thicker and the Au@AgPt NSs will form agglomeration, which would cause the transmission spectrum to change and affect the RI sensitivity of Au@AgPt NSs. Hence, the RI sensitivities of different grow times of Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure have been optimized (see Fig. S1), 23 s was selected for the optimized grow time of Au@AgPt NSs on the APTES-modified fiber. Fig. 3(b) illustrates the transmission change (ΔT) as a function of RI value. ΔT was defined as T0-Tn, where T0 was the transmission intensity value after the fiber functionalization with Au@AgPt NSs in water (nwater = 1.3330), and Tn was the transmission intensity value after the fiber functionalization with Au@AgPt NSs in different glycerol-water mixed solution. The RI sensitivity is defined as Sn = ΔT /Δn, where n is the refractive index of the solution. When the RI changed from 1.3330 to 1.3910, the sensitivity of the Au@AgPt NSs was measured as 633 %/RIU. The transmission intensity of Au @ AgPt NSs is not only affected by the attenuation length of the local electric field enhancement, but also by the imaginary part of the dielectric function of Pt and the mass fraction of Pt in the AgPt alloy shell, which will cause the damping of plasma exciton peak [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure for the quantitative and selective detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylenimine (PEI) in this article is a water-soluble linear macromolecular polymer, which contains a large amount of amino N atoms in the molecular chain. Amino groups can react with Cu2+ to form a relatively stable chelate system [24]. The positively charged PEI was successively adsorbed onto the surface of the negatively charged Au@AgPt NSs using electrostatic adsorption. The low-resolution SEM images of PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified MMF-SMF-MMF structure is shown in Fig. 4 (a), Fig. 4(b) shows the high-resolution SEM image of Fig. 4(a). When PEI was modified on the surface of Au@AgPt NSs, the SEM image appears in a fuzzy state due to the non-conductivity of the polymer, which further confirms the electrostatic interaction between PEI and Au@AgPt NSs.

In order to measure the real depth of the transmission peak at 564 nm related to the stronger binding capability between N-containing ligands in PEI and Cu2+, the PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified hetero-core structured optical fiber was immersed in different Cu2+ concentrations ranging from 10−16 to 10-10 mol/L. A washing step in sodium citrate-citrate buffer solution (pH = 4) was necessary prior to recording the next measurement. It needs to remove unreacted or unstable Cu2+, and then their respective LSPR transmission spectra were recorded in the citrate buffer sodium (0.1 mol/l, pH = 4) after the Cu2+ detection. Fig. 4(c) depicts the LSPR variation with Cu2+ ion concentration and it manifests as a pure intensity change across the transmission spectrum. As the concentration of Cu2+ increases, the depth of the transmission peak at 564 nm increasing gradually until reaching a saturation value when the Cu2+ concentration exceeded 10-11 mol/L. When Cu2+ comes into the surrounding and binds with the PEI, the real part of the RI of the PEI-Au@AgPt NSs layer increases. This change in RI was recorded by observing change in resonance intensity value while satisfying the resonance condition. In the case that the amount of PEI imobilised on the Au@AgPt NSs surface was lower and the number of binding sites were finite, this would lead to a saturation state for higher concentration values of Cu2+, thus no further change in RI of the PEI-Au@AgPt NSs layer occurs.

Fig. 4(d) illustrates the transmission change (ΔT) as a function of Cu2+ concentration. ΔT was defined as T0-T564, where T0 was the resonance intensity value at 564 nm measured after the fiber functionalization with PEI-Au@AgPt NSs in sodium citrate-citrate buffer solution (0.1 mol/L, pH = 4), and T564 was the resonance intensity value at 564 nm in the presence of Cu2+ for each Cu2+ concentration tested. The values of T564 for different Cu2+concentrations were extracted from the transmittance spectra shown in Fig. 4(c). We used three sensors for statistics evaluation, each sensor in this article was only used once for one sensing experiment. The data were fitted using the following equation with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.998:
Δ T = 1.050 + 0.454
l n
C
where C is the Cu2+ concentration in fmol/L. The calibration curve is characterized by an excellent linear response which is a requirement for the vast majority of existing biochemical sensing systems. This study has confirmed that PEI has a good affinity with Cu2+ and PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified hetero-core structured optical fiber which has a high RI sensitivity to the change of local dielectric environment. The proposed PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified hetero-core structured fiber sensor shows the highest sensitivity (10−16 mol/L) of Cu2+ detection reported so far in Table 1 , which has potentially significant implications for the quantitative detection of Cu2+ in biological fields.

The selectivity of metal ions is a key index for evaluating the specificity of metal chelators and is of great significance for the quantitative detection of metal ions in the environment and in living systems. Interferences from other metal ions including Mg2+, Mn2+, Sn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe3+ and Zn2+ were studied under the same experimental condition as that for Cu2+ detection. LSPR transmittance spectra show that negligible responses when treated with other metal ions at a concentration of 10−13 mol/L in sodium citrate-citrate buffer solution at pH 4.0 (Fig. 5 ). This result indicates that the PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified hetero-core structured optical fiber has a high selectivity for Cu2+ detection relative to other metal ions, which is owing to the stronger binding capability between N-containing ligands and Cu2+ [13,45]. Qiu et al. reported that the chemisorptions between Cu2+ and amino groups was a rapid kinetic process [46]. Wang et al. have found that the adsorption coefficient (adsorption coefficient of 103 ml/g is considered good and that above 104 ml/g is considered outstanding) between the Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2 nanoparticles and Cu2+ was 104 ml/g at 25℃ [45]. Hu et al. reported that Cu2+ possesses a higher absolute electronegativity than other metal ions, and the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atoms would also coordinate Cu2+ ions to form more stable complexes, leading to the excellent selectivity of Cu2+ ions [13]. Hence, the high selectivity of the proposed sensor is mainly attributed to not only the chelation between amino groups in PEI and Cu2+, but also the stronger attraction between the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atoms in PEI and Cu2+.

In summary, this article describes for the first time that trimetallic nanoparticles-modified optical fiber for the accurate measurement of Cu2+. An efficient approach for synthesizing citrate-stabilized Au@AgPt core-shell nanospheres (NSs) with a discontinuous AgPt alloy shell has been demonstrated. The formation of which is owing to the synergistic effect of galvanic replacement between Ag and H2PtCl6·6H2O and reduction reaction between Na3Ct and H2PtCl6·6H2O under 100 °C. The Au@AgPt NSs modified hetero-core structured fiber optic exhibited the highest RI sensitivity (633 %/RIU). PEI was successively adsorbed on the optimized Au@AgPt NSs surface using electrostatic adsorption for the quantitative measurement of Cu2+. A highly linear response to Cu2+ concentration in the range 10−16 to 10-11 mol/L was achieved. The highly sensitivity and good selectivity are mainly attributed to the strong affinity of PEI with Cu2+ and the plasmonic coupling effect of Au@AgPt NSs to the changes of local dielectric environment. The proposed PEI-Au@AgPt NSs modified hetero-core structured fiber sensor shows the highest sensitivity (0.1 fmol/L) of Cu2+ detection reported so far in a wide range of environmental monitoring and biosensing application.

Qing Huang: Writing - review & editing. Wenjie Zhu: Writing - review & editing. Yong Wang: Writing - review & editing. Zhao Deng: Writing - review & editing. Zhi Li: Writing - review & editing. Jiankun Peng: Writing - review & editing. Dajuan Lyu: Writing - review & editing. Elfed Lewis: Writing - review & editing. Minghong Yang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, NSFC (Project Number: 61575151). Prof. Elfed Lewis is supported by the Science Foundation Ireland under the MaREI Centre research programme (Grant SFI/12/RC/2302_P2). We thank Dr. Zhao Deng and Dr. Ting-ting Luo for the help with TEM/HRTEM and STEM EDS characterizations, we also thank Xiao-kun Wu for participating in the preparation of the fiber-optic sensing probe.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128480.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Behari, V. Pardasani, Genetics of Wilsons disease, Parkinsonism Relat. D. 16 (2010) 639-644.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Altered copper status in adult men with cystic fibrosis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cyclam-functionalized carbon dots sensor for sensitive and selective detection of copper(II) ion and sulfide anion in aqueous media and its imaging in live cells

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Minghong Yang received his PhD in physics from Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China, in 2003. From 2003 to 2005, he worked as a postdoctoral fellow at the Fraunhofer Institute for Applied Optics and Fine Mechanics, Jena, Germany. From 2006 to 2008, he worked as a research fellow at Berlin University of Technology, where he dealt with thin film sensors. He is now a research scientist in the National Engineering Laboratory for Optical Fiber Sensors at Wuhan University of Technology, China. His research interest is thin film optical fiber sensors.
